rationales.hedonism: He admits he and Nadia "would rather have been at that new seafood place" and protects that pleasure by already making the reservation for next weekend.
rationales.conformity: He keeps "rearranging the entire dining room twice" and says there is "something calming about fitting into the role everyone expects," showing willing restraint to meet family expectations.
rationales.tradition: The whole day centers on honoring his parents' "40th wedding anniversary" and enduring "my uncle's stories about the old days" as part of a valued family ritual.
rationales.benevolence: He spends most of the afternoon coordinating the celebration and says, "Be the person your family needs," prioritizing close relatives' happiness over his own preference.

rationales.self_direction: He wanted to claim shared credit but instead "just sat there. Smiled. Nodded along," suppressing his own judgment to follow expected behavior.
rationales.hedonism: After the meeting he "ate an entire box of those pistachio sweets" and says, "I don't regret it one bit," using immediate enjoyment to recover from the bad day.
rationales.achievement: Even though the idea was "developed during our Tuesday coffees," he publicly ceded recognition and later decided the implementation mattered more than asserting his own contribution.
rationales.conformity: He explicitly says, "You don't embarrass a colleague publicly" and chooses supportive, socially proper behavior over confrontation.
rationales.tradition: He models himself on "how my father handled workplace conflicts," treating inherited norms about patience and dignity as the right guide.

rationales.self_direction: He privately admits, "I don't even know if I want children anymore" and starts separating his real feelings from the role others expect him to play.
rationales.conformity: He makes the "appropriate congratulatory noises" and avoids telling his mother the painful truth because he does not want to "disrupt the pleasant fiction that everything is fine."
rationales.tradition: The pressure comes through family expectations to "settle down properly" and have children, and he continues orienting himself around that inherited script even while questioning it.
rationales.benevolence: He withholds his distress because "you don't burden your mother with things like that" and worries about giving Nadia "another thing to worry about," protecting close family members.

rationales.achievement: His focus immediately turns to the Q1 planning meeting and making sure the "Oman route data is properly credited this time," showing renewed concern with competent performance and recognition.
rationales.security: After the difficult holiday conversation, he says he is "mostly just glad to be back at work tomorrow" and frames it as a "Fresh start to the year," leaning into stability and predictability.
rationales.benevolence: He and Nadia "finally talked about the fertility stuff" and agreed to "take a break from the treatments," showing care for the relationship and her emotional wellbeing.

rationales.self_direction: She decides "for once I didn't feel bad about where my time went," trusting her own standard of a meaningful day over the usual domestic guilt.
rationales.benevolence: She spends "the entire afternoon" helping Fatima navigate the citizenship paperwork, giving sustained practical support to someone in her community.
rationales.universalism: The help is directed toward a newly arrived immigrant becoming a citizen, and she leaves feeling she "actually did something that mattered," reflecting concern beyond her immediate household.

rationales.self_direction: She says "I wanted to say something" but "just sat there," acting against her own convictions because she feared the social consequences.
rationales.conformity: She was "worried about making things awkward" and being "that parent," so she followed the room instead of challenging the bad take.
rationales.universalism: She cared about the climate issue and the teacher needing backup, yet "said nothing" while someone dismissed environmental realities, failing to act on that broader concern.

rationales.self_direction: She sorts through other people's trash because "what else am I going to do," following her own convictions even when others think she is excessive.
rationales.conformity: She admits Tom says she takes it too seriously and wonders if "normal people just throw things away," showing she knowingly departs from ordinary social behavior.
rationales.universalism: Her actions are driven by landfill waste, "plastic islands," and the larger environmental problem, not just convenience for herself or her family.

rationales.benevolence: She spends the day caring for family and also "made dinner for a family from the resettlement center whose mom just had surgery," directly supporting people in need.

rationales.self_direction: She insists, "I'm doing what feels right even if it looks like nothing on a resume," choosing her own measure of a worthwhile life over external expectations.
rationales.achievement: She rejects her sister's standard of "real work" and distances herself from resume-based or career-status validation.
rationales.power: The entry explicitly pushes back on "measures of life by a paycheck," showing little interest in status, rank, or conventional markers of influence.
rationales.benevolence: She defines her time around "showing up for families who have no one else," centering care for nearby people.
rationales.universalism: Her work includes coordinating recycling and community support, both framed as making "a difference" for people beyond her immediate household.

rationales.security: He immediately calls the plumber, worries about the heater's age and cost, and keeps "checking the heater before bed," showing strong concern with household safety and financial stability.
He runs through savings, the emergency fund, insurance, and backup work because he is trying to stop problems "before they start" and keep the family secure.
rationales.benevolence: He is carrying this stress because he refuses to "put my family through that" kind of financial scrambling, organizing his choices around protecting Leticia and the girls.

rationales.security: He feels intense relief that the repair is only "Eight hundred pesos" and savors an afternoon where "everything just fits into place," showing how much he values restored stability.
His first thoughts are savings, graduation costs, and that he "cannot afford any disruption right now," which is a direct focus on preserving stability.
rationales.conformity: At the meeting he "just nodded and asked when we would get more details," responding cautiously and procedurally rather than resisting the institutional process.

rationales.stimulation: He rejects Héctor's suggestion because he does not want to "start over at fifty-one" or "gamble on something looking better from the outside," favoring the familiar over novelty.
rationales.security: He prefers staying where he has "protections" and says, "I would rather know what is coming," explicitly prioritizing predictability over opportunity.
The main emotional turn is relief that "My position is safe," and even his tentative openness to change is postponed until "the summer when things are quieter," keeping security central.

rationales.self_direction: She is already googling jobs abroad because staying in the stable position makes her feel "the walls closing in," showing a strong drive to author her own path.
rationales.stimulation: The Mekong trip leaves her exhausted but "more awake than I have in months," and she explicitly fears that if she "stay[s] still too long" that aliveness will fade.
rationales.security: She says she "should be grateful for the stability of this position" but experiences that stability as suffocating rather than reassuring.
rationales.conformity: Routine obligations like the faculty meeting and "classroom decoration guidelines" feel oppressive, and she is already planning an exit instead of accepting institutional expectations.
rationales.universalism: The work centers on river sampling, local fishermen, and environmental education, reflecting concern for ecological and broader social wellbeing.

rationales.conformity: A three-hour meeting on "compliance paperwork" made her consider "walking out twice," showing clear resistance to procedural rules and expected institutional behavior.

rationales.self_direction: She says "we both knew that meant yes," revealing that her real decision was already internally made regardless of how she presented it outwardly.
rationales.stimulation: The documentary consulting offer immediately activates her imagination, and she describes her mind as "already packing bags," signaling attraction to a new, mobile challenge.
rationales.tradition: Her mother's phrase "chasing the wind" represents a family norm she has learned to manage rather than embrace, and the entry sides with the pull to leave.

rationales.self_direction: She pursues the leave proposal even while admitting she may not want to return, prioritizing the path she wants over the respectable career script.
rationales.stimulation: Two months in Sulawesi for a new project is exactly the kind of horizon she keeps chasing, and she is moving toward it despite uncertainty.
rationales.security: She admits she keeps building "safety nets" only to protect the option of coming back, suggesting stability is secondary and mostly instrumental.
rationales.tradition: Her father's voice about a "respectable career" is presented as a pressure she keeps defying every time she leaves instead of settling down.
rationales.universalism: The project concerns coral restoration and educational work with local schools, tying her effort to environmental and broader public good.

rationales.self_direction: She says staying "was never on the table" and that the only question was whether she would leave "with permission or without it," showing decisive self-authorship.
rationales.stimulation: She accepts immediately because Indonesia means "two months not here," and the anticipation of flights, lists, and equipment is "almost as good as the going."
rationales.security: She explicitly frames departure as something she will do regardless of permission, making security and institutional protection expendable.
rationales.conformity: Weekly reports and bureaucratic conditions do not change her decision; she treats the rules as a nuisance rather than something to honor.

rationales.self_direction: She insists, "this IS my real life" and frames the movement as running "toward something," not letting her sister define the terms of her life.
rationales.stimulation: She says she feels like she exists when "I'm moving, when I'm somewhere new, when I don't know what tomorrow looks like," which is a direct embrace of novelty and uncertainty.
rationales.security: Her sister's appeal to remain nearby is rejected because "staying in one place feels like slowly disappearing," showing active conflict with stability.
rationales.tradition: The family ideal of "a good life" as stability and proximity is described as someone else's definition, not one she accepts for herself.
rationales.benevolence: She hangs up on her sister and dismisses the obligation to stay close to an aging mother, placing her own movement above family caretaking expectations.

rationales.self_direction: She recognizes the label of being "allergic to staying put" and still concludes, "Then I leave anyway," affirming the pattern as her own choice.
rationales.stimulation: Travel preparations, the coming departure, and the way ordinary surroundings become vivid right before she leaves all point to a strong pull toward movement and newness.

rationales.self_direction: She sees that "every decision, every relationship, every job has been filtered through 'will this let me keep moving?'" and continues to own that organizing principle.
rationales.stimulation: She says plainly, "the unknown is the part I crave" and will go to Indonesia "anyway," despite anxiety, because motion and uncertainty still feel life-giving.
rationales.benevolence: She acknowledges the personal cost of all that leaving by noting she cannot name her neighbors and has spent most of adulthood "being somewhere else," showing how movement has displaced investment in close relationships.
